subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Federal Government vs. States in Ensuring Educational Opportunities
stimulus: The federal government and the states each play an important role in public education. Take a position on whether the federal government or the states are more effective in ensuring educational opportunities for all students.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Fourteenth Amendment of the United States Constitution, Tenth Amendment of the United States Constitution, Article I of the United States Constitution.)
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row A — Thesis/Claim): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis establishing a line of reasoning about whether the federal government or states are more effective in ensuring educational opportunities.

Examples that earn the point: 'The federal government is more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because the federal government has more resources to address issues of inequality than do individual states.' OR 'The federal government is more effective because it can create national policies that apply to all states.' OR 'State governments are more effective because states are closer to citizens and can develop solutions to fit particular state issues.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about there being a debate do not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
rubric_criteria: 0 pts: No accurate evidence, or evidence not relevant to the topic.
1 pt (Row B — 1 point): One piece of evidence relevant to the topic.
2 pts (Row B — 2 points): One piece of specific and relevant evidence supports the claim (requires Row A).
3 pts (Row B — 3 points): Two pieces of specific and relevant evidence, one from a listed document (Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, or Article I) (requires Row A).

Examples of acceptable evidence: 'The Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection clause supports federal intervention to ensure equal educational opportunities.' 'The Tenth Amendment reserves powers not delegated to the federal government to the states, supporting state control of education.' 'Article I gives Congress the power to spend for the general welfare, enabling federal education funding programs.' 'Brown v. Board of Education required federal intervention to end segregation in schools.' 'Laboratories of democracy allow states to innovate and develop effective local solutions.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, must have earned Row A. To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must come from the Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, or Article I.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row C — Reasoning): Explains the relationship between evidence and the claim.

Examples that earn the point: 'Without federal intervention through the Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection guarantee, states would be free to discriminate in educational access, as they did before Brown v. Board of Education.' 'The Tenth Amendment's reservation of powers to states reflects the founders' belief that local governance is more responsive, which is why states are better positioned to tailor education policy to community needs.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain why the evidence supports the specific claim.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row D — Alternate Perspective): Responds to an opposing perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. Must describe an alternate perspective AND engage with it.

Examples that earn the point: 'While some argue that states are better because they are closer to citizens and understand local needs, this overlooks the persistent inequalities between wealthy and low-income states that only federal funding and oversight can address.' 'Even though the federal government has more resources, its one-size-fits-all approach fails to account for the diverse needs of different communities — state-level control is essential for effective educational policy.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.
